title: Request Authority, Host, Target Reconstruction, and Routing · Execution Trace: Effective Request Uri
summary: A server trusts any Host, accepts duplicate authority sources, reconstructs links from forwarded input, routes before validating the target, and confuses TLS identity with request authority. Planned artifact: Add and verify the effective request uri increment in authority validation and target reconstruction table. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Reconstruct the effective request URI from target form, request-target, Host, connection security, and trusted configuration.
Require exactly one valid Host field for HTTP/1.1 where applicable and reject missing, empty, duplicate, or invalid authority.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Effective Request Uri · Theory Effective Request Uri
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 6896 in a incident replay lab retaining minimized request-smuggling corpora. Build deterministic Go evidence to derive and allowlist one effective request authority without trusting disagreement; preserve this operating constraint: no malformed framing may be normalized and forwarded to another recipient; then reject a protocol transition while optimistic bytes are buffered and prevent desynchronization. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Reconstruct the effective request URI from target form, request-target, Host, connection security, and trusted configuration.

steps.title: Build Effective Request Uri evidence · Theory Effective Request Uri
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: httpproto-effective-request-uri-k62kn1" that uses case 4277 to derive and allowlist one effective request authority without trusting disagreement. Browser code must not open sockets or listeners, negotiate TLS, contact networks or proxies, capture packets, read host state, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with the named Go 1.26 protocol transfer gates.
